File Structure

dataset/
├── 00_raw_datasets/
│   ├── inference_offline_llama3_70b/         # LLaMA-3 70B offline batch inference
│   ├── inference_online_finite_llama3_70b/   # Online inference, finite request pool
│   ├── inference_online_rate_llama3_70b/     # Online inference, Poisson arrival
│   ├── training_llama2_70b_lora/             # LLaMA-2 70B LoRA fine-tuning
│   │   ├── 2node/ 4node/ 8node/ 16node/
│   └── training_stable_diffusion/            # Stable Diffusion image gen training
│       ├── 2node/ 4node/ 8node/ 16node/
├── 01_aggregated_datasets/                   # Pre-aggregated Parquet files (0.2 s)
│   ├── training/                   41 files  (llama2_lora: 21, stable_diff: 20)
│   ├── inference_offline_llama3_70b/  1,200 files
│   ├── inference_online_finite_llama3_70b/  1,026 files
│   └── inference_online_rate_llama3_70b/     200 files
├── 02_analysis_scripts/
└── 03_whole-facility_profiles/
Data provenance: All power measurements are real hardware measurements from NVIDIA H100 GPUs using NVML (GPU) and Intel RAPL (CPU) via the WattAMeter tool. Data collected on an HPC cluster running production-scale GenAI workloads.

Phase 2: Basic Statistical Analysis

Note: Training stats are per-node (GPU+CPU). Inference stats are per-node (single node). Per-GPU values = node power ÷ 4.
Workload Mean_W Median_W Max_W Min_W Std_W CV_% P95_W P99_W
LLaMA2-LoRA (2node) 658.48 647.01 768.06 106.93 134.78 20.47 754.04 755.64
LLaMA2-LoRA (4node) 639.66 656.55 764.41 102.85 159.17 24.88 753.80 755.08
LLaMA2-LoRA (8node) 611.54 610.72 757.50 104.13 181.33 29.65 753.42 754.43
LLaMA2-LoRA (16node) 597.48 609.83 756.75 93.73 198.22 33.18 751.90 752.88
StableDiff (2node) 657.02 708.82 751.81 103.63 148.36 22.58 743.70 744.78
StableDiff (4node) 523.39 607.57 745.74 105.27 215.49 41.17 740.66 741.92
StableDiff (8node) 503.94 597.47 744.05 105.19 211.88 42.04 741.69 742.64
StableDiff (16node) 558.67 606.59 743.65 103.16 163.92 29.34 741.86 742.87
Inf-Online-Rate (per trace) 517.39 518.36 524.46 318.77 13.53 2.62 521.67 522.36
Inf-Offline (per trace) 705.53 712.37 763.44 198.04 50.14 7.11 756.50 760.02
Inf-Online-Finite (per trace) 521.33 563.97 587.92 186.86 118.47 22.72 578.01 582.33

Interpretation

  • Most stable: LLaMA-2 LoRA training – CV ≈ 33.2% reflects the highly periodic nature of transformer training.
  • Most variable: Inference workloads – power fluctuates strongly with request batch sizes and KV cache occupancy.
  • Node scaling: Per-GPU power is approximately constant across node counts (2N→16N), confirming good parallel efficiency.

Phase 3: Multi-Scale Temporal Analysis

Key Findings by Timescale

TimescaleObservationPower Systems Implication
0.2 s (raw)Sharp transitions at batch boundaries; checkpoint drops visibleUPS must handle sub-second transients continuously
1 s (MA)High-freq noise attenuated; periodic pattern emergesStatic VAR compensators operate at this scale
10 s (MA)Batch periodicity smoothed; checkpoint drops still visibleBESS can target this timescale for smoothing
60 s (MA)Near-constant baseload with occasional step changes at checkpointsIdeal for AGC (Automatic Generation Control)

Phase 4: Checkpoint Event Analysis

Checkpoint mechanism: During model checkpointing, GPU computation is interrupted while tensors are serialized and written to storage. This causes a characteristic power drop that is detectable in the 0.2 s resolution data.

Detected Events Summary

Workload Threshold_% N_events Mean_duration_s Mean_drop_% Mean_interval_s
LLaMA2-LoRA 5 161 7.37 15.64 12.84
LLaMA2-LoRA 10 161 5.05 16.74 12.84
LLaMA2-LoRA 15 24 17.56 20.97 89.35
StableDiff 5 5 40.96 63.55 344.25
StableDiff 10 5 39.24 65.89 344.30
StableDiff 15 5 38.52 67.33 344.40

Engineering Significance

  • Duration: ~5.0 s per event – confirms fast NVMe or parallel file system checkpointing
  • Interval: ~13 s between events – corresponds to typical save_steps in LoRA training configs
  • Drop magnitude: ~16.7% below baseline – measurable but not extreme
  • Predictability: Regular interval enables proactive grid demand scheduling
  • UPS opportunity: Each checkpoint provides a ~5 s window where UPS can transfer load and recharge

Phase 5: Frequency Domain Analysis

Dominant Frequency Findings

Workload Freq_Hz Period_s PSD_amplitude
LLaMA2-LoRA 16N 0.00 518.60 10605904549.71
LLaMA2-LoRA 16N 0.09 10.58 4209159070.43
LLaMA2-LoRA 16N 0.29 3.50 358238002.87
StableDiff 16N 0.00 350.60 4683711534.52
StableDiff 16N 0.07 13.48 442255967.97
StableDiff 16N 0.15 6.62 299199009.69
StableDiff 16N 0.03 38.96 671963430.86
StableDiff 16N 0.23 4.44 209035041.05
Inf-Online-Rate 0.12 8.19 5850.89

Interpretation

Training (LLaMA-2 LoRA)

  • Dominant peak corresponds to batch/step period (compute + communication)
  • Sub-harmonic at checkpoint interval
  • Very low power at high frequencies → nearly deterministic process

Inference (Online Rate)

  • Broader spectrum → stochastic request arrivals
  • No strong single frequency → variable load
  • Power below ~0.01 Hz = quasi-static, above → bursty
Grid relevance: The dominant training frequencies (likely 0.1–1 Hz) are in the range where grid frequency regulators do not act but power electronics (inverters, UPS) must respond. This represents a novel class of power quality challenge not covered by existing standards.

Grid Integration Classification

WorkloadLoad TypeScheduling FlexibilityPower Quality Challenge

Fine-tuning (SD)✅ Base LoadHigh (shorter runs)Similar to training
Inf-Offline⚡ FlexibleVery High (batch deferrable)Low – near-constant during batch
Inf-Online-Finite⚡ VariableLow (latency-sensitive)High – traffic-driven fluctuations
Inf-Online-Rate⚡ VariableLow (real-time)Highest – Poisson arrivals

Phase 7: Data Center Scale-up Analysis

Scenario Correlation N_GPUs Mean_GW Peak_GW Std_GW Peak_to_Average CV_%
Scenario A – 25,000 H100 Fully Sync (ρ=1) 25000 0.01 0.02 0.01 1.44 46.80
Scenario A – 25,000 H100 Independent (ρ=0) 25000 0.01 0.01 0.00 1.01 0.29
Scenario A – 25,000 H100 50% Corr (ρ=0.5) 25000 0.01 0.02 0.00 1.31 33.09
Scenario B – 100,000 H100 Fully Sync (ρ=1) 100000 0.05 0.08 0.02 1.44 46.80
Scenario B – 100,000 H100 Independent (ρ=0) 100000 0.05 0.05 0.00 1.01 0.15
Scenario B – 100,000 H100 50% Corr (ρ=0.5) 100000 0.05 0.07 0.02 1.31 33.09
Scenario C – 1 GW DC Fully Sync (ρ=1) 1673687 0.88 1.27 0.41 1.44 46.80
Scenario C – 1 GW DC Independent (ρ=0) 1673687 0.88 0.88 0.00 1.00 0.04
Scenario C – 1 GW DC 50% Corr (ρ=0.5) 1673687 0.88 1.15 0.29 1.31 33.09
⚠ Critical Finding – Synchronized Training Amplification: When all GPUs execute the same training step synchronously, power fluctuations scale linearly with GPU count. A 1 GW synchronized AI data center could exhibit ramp events of tens of MW within sub-second timescales – exceeding the response capability of conventional grid protection systems.

Aggregation Effect by Correlation

Correlation ModelPhysical InterpretationCV Impact
Fully Synchronized (ρ=1)All jobs on same scheduler tick, same data parallelism stepCV = per-GPU CV (no reduction)
Partially Correlated (ρ=0.5)Mixed workloads, some job staggeringCV reduced by ~30%
Fully Independent (ρ=0)Random job arrivals, perfectly staggered checkpointsCV → 0 as N→∞ (√N smoothing)

Phase 8: Power Engineering Implications

🔋 UPS Design

  • Size for P99 ramp rate: 17358 W/node/s
  • Checkpoint valleys (~5 s) enable recharge windows
  • Prefer Li-Ion for high-cycle training applications
  • Sub-10 ms switching time remains critical for fault events

⚡ BESS Sizing

  • Target 1–60 s smoothing window
  • Supercapacitors for sub-1 s; Li-Ion for 1–60 s
  • 30 s BESS for 100K-GPU cluster ≈ 0.1 MWh
  • Checkpoint-aware dispatch saves 15–25% BESS cycling

🌐 Grid Frequency Regulation

  • Checkpoint events → predictable demand steps for TSO
  • Synchronized training = new class of industrial load
  • GW-scale DCs should provide mandatory demand response
  • Consider AI DCs as grid-forming assets via on-site generation

🏗️ Future GW-Scale AI DCs

  • Peak provisioning: 1266–879 MW depending on sync
  • New interconnection standards needed for correlated loads
  • Stagger job scheduling across clusters to reduce grid impact
  • Real-time power telemetry to grid operator is essential
Research Gaps Identified: (1) Multi-DC correlation effects when hyperscalers co-locate training jobs across campuses; (2) Interaction of AI DC checkpoint loads with renewable generation intermittency; (3) Optimal BESS + demand response co-optimization for synchronized training clusters; (4) Grid code requirements for facilities with sub-second GW-scale load variability.

Analysis based on real hardware measurements from Vercellino et al. (2026), arXiv:2604.07345.

Figures generated with matplotlib | Statistics via numpy/scipy | Data processing via pandas
